clinical_profile.introduction_synonyms: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic, systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily characterised by inflammation of the synovial joints. It commonly affects the small joints of the hands and feet in a symmetrical pattern, leading to pain, swelling, stiffness, and progressive joint destruction. The abbreviation RA is frequently used in medical literature and by healthcare professionals.
clinical_profile.clinical_presentation_and_symptoms: Patients typically present with persistent pain, swelling, and tenderness in multiple joints, most often affecting the metacarpophalangeal, proximal interphalangeal, and metatarsophalangeal joints, often symmetrically. A hallmark symptom is morning stiffness lasting for more than 30 minutes, which improves with activity. Other common symptoms include fatigue, malaise, low-grade fever, and unintentional weight loss. Some patients may also develop extra-articular manifestations such as rheumatoid nodules, dry eyes, or pleurisy.
clinical_profile.assessment_diagnosis: Assessment by medical professionals typically begins with a comprehensive clinical history, focusing on the pattern, duration, and severity of joint symptoms, as well as any systemic symptoms. A physical examination assesses joint swelling, tenderness, range of motion, and any evidence of deformity or extra-articular manifestations. Diagnosis is informed by laboratory tests, including inflammatory markers such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), and autoantibodies such as rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA or anti-CCP). Imaging studies, primarily X-rays, are used to assess for joint erosions and joint space narrowing, while ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can detect earlier signs of synovitis and bone oedema. The American College of Rheumatology/European League Against Rheumatism (ACR/EULAR) 2010 classification criteria are commonly used to support the diagnosis.
clinical_profile.therapeutic_plan_treatment: The initial therapeutic plan for rheumatoid arthritis typically involves prompt initiation of conventional synthetic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (csDMARDs), with methotrexate being the cornerstone of treatment, often combined with a short course of glucocorticoids to control acute inflammation. Other commonly used csDMARDs include sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine, which may be used as monotherapy or in combination. If initial treatment with csDMARDs is insufficient to achieve disease control, treatment is escalated to biological disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (bDMARDs), such as tumour necrosis factor (TNF) inhibitors (e.g., etanercept, adalimumab), or other biologics targeting specific immune pathways (e.g., rituximab, abatacept, tocilizumab). Targeted synthetic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (tsDMARDs), such as Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors, are also used when csDMARDs are inadequate. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and additional glucocorticoids may be used for symptomatic relief, but they do not modify the disease course.
clinical_profile.complications_prognosis: Common complications seen among people diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis include progressive joint damage leading to erosions, deformities, and loss of joint function. Extra-articular complications can include cardiovascular disease, interstitial lung disease, osteoporosis, Sjögren's syndrome, anaemia, carpal tunnel syndrome, rheumatoid nodules, and vasculitis. Without effective treatment, the short-term prognosis involves persistent pain and disability, while the medium to longer-term prognosis can lead to severe joint destruction, significant functional impairment, and reduced quality of life. With early diagnosis and aggressive treatment using modern therapies, many patients can achieve disease remission or low disease activity, thereby improving their prognosis, reducing joint damage, and preserving physical function.
clinical_profile.disqualifiers: Medical professionals must consider several differential diagnoses when assessing for rheumatoid arthritis. These include other inflammatory arthritides such as psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, systemic lupus erythematosus (lupus arthritis), gout, pseudogout, and reactive arthritis. Septic arthritis and viral arthritis also need to be considered, especially in acute presentations. Osteoarthritis is another important differential, although it typically affects different joints, is less symmetrical, and lacks the systemic inflammatory features and specific autoantibodies of rheumatoid arthritis. Differentiation is crucial, and some conditions, such as gout or septic arthritis, require immediate and distinct management. The initial diagnostic workup typically aims to distinguish rheumatoid arthritis from these other conditions concurrently.
clinical_profile.epidemiology: The prevalence and incidence of rheumatoid arthritis are generally higher in females than in males, typically with an onset in middle age, although it can manifest at any age. Non-modifiable risk factors include genetic predisposition, particularly specific human leukocyte antigen-DRB1 (HLA-DRB1) alleles. Modifiable risk factors include smoking, obesity, and periodontal disease. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis commonly experience comorbidities such as cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, interstitial lung disease, Sjögren's syndrome, anaemia, and depression, which can precede or follow the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis.
